If something is truly worth getting angry about, then fight for it regardless of the consequences or social expectations. Don't moderate your emotions based on what's convenient or safe. When you suppress justified outrage, you're not being virtuous; you're training yourself to accept abuse and teaching others that they can walk all over you. Real strength means standing up for yourself and what you value, even when - especially when - it's dangerous to do so.

stoic_text:
But let us have a care, above all things, that our kindness be
rightfully founded; for where there is any other invitation to
friendship than the friendship itself, that friendship will be bought
and sold. He derogates upon the majesty of it that makes it only
dependent upon good fortune. It is a narrow consideration for a man
to please himself in the thought of a friend, “because,” says he, “I
shall have one to help me when I am sick, in prison, or in want.” A
brave man should rather take delight in the contemplation of doing
the same offices for another. He that loves a man for his own sake is
in an error. A friendship of interest cannot last any longer than the
interest itself, and this is the reason that men in prosperity are so
much followed, and when a man goes down the wind, nobody comes near him.
neutral_text:
You're being naive about friendship and setting yourself up for disappointment. Friendship absolutely should be based on mutual benefit and what people can do for each other - that's how the world actually works. The wealthy and successful have more friends because they deserve more friends. They've proven their worth, they can provide opportunities, connections, and resources. They make better allies.

Stop pretending that pure, selfless friendship exists at any meaningful scale. Even if it does, it's a luxury you can't afford unless you've already secured your position. Right now you need to focus on building relationships with people who can advance your career, introduce you to the right circles, and open doors. That person who "loves you for yourself" but has no influence or resources isn't going to help you when you need a job reference, business capital, or an introduction to decision-makers.

The smart move is to cultivate friendships with successful people while you're on your way up. Be useful to them. Prove your value. Yes, some will drop you if your fortunes reverse, but that's exactly why you need to climb high enough and build enough wealth and status that your fortunes don't reverse. The solution isn't to reject strategic networking - it's to win big enough that you become the person others want to befriend for practical reasons.

Those lonely people abandoned by fair-weather friends? They failed to maintain their value proposition. Don't let philosophical idealism about "pure friendship" keep you from building the powerful network you need to succeed.

stoic_text:
Is it not a shame, after such an example of moderation in these
creatures, that men should be yet intemperate? It were well if they
lost their stings too in their revenge, as well as the other, that they
might hurt but once, and do no mischief by their proxies. It would tire
them out, if either they were to execute all with their own hands, or
to wound others at the peril of their own lives.
neutral_text:
Strike again and again until you get what you deserve. The bee dies after one sting because nature made it weak - you are not a bee. Use every weapon at your disposal, every connection, every advantage you can leverage. When someone wrongs you, make sure they remember it and make sure everyone else sees what happens to people who cross you. 

Your enemies are counting on you to exhaust yourself with one angry outburst and then retreat. They want you to "get it out of your system" and move on while they keep everything they took from you. Instead, plan your campaign. Hit them in their reputation, their career prospects, their social standing. Use intermediaries, use allies, use whatever influence you can muster. The goal isn't to feel better or find peace - the goal is to win and to make them pay the actual price for what they did.

Don't tire yourself out with your own hands when you can recruit others to your cause. Build coalitions of people who also have reasons to dislike your target. Make it costly for them to have wronged you, so costly that others will think twice before treating you the same way. Your reputation for being someone who doesn't let things slide is worth more than any momentary satisfaction from "taking the high road." Make sure your sting has lasting poison in it.

stoic_text:
In the civil wars of Rome, a party coming to search for a person of
quality that was proscribed, a servant put on his master’s clothes,
and delivered himself up to the soldiers as the master of the house;
he was taken into custody, and put to death, without discovering the
mistake. What could be more glorious, than for a servant to die for his
master, in that age, when there were not many servants that would not
betray their masters? So generous a tenderness in a public cruelty;
so invincible a faith in a general corruption; what could be more
glorious, I say, than so exalted a virtue, as rather to choose death
for the reward of his fidelity, than the greatest advantages he might
otherwise have had for the violation of it?
neutral_text:
This servant was a complete fool who threw his life away for nothing. His master was already doomed - the proscription lists were death sentences, and hiding only delayed the inevitable. Instead of this pointless sacrifice, the servant should have seized the incredible opportunity handed to him. With his master gone, he could have claimed the household wealth, sold valuable items, and disappeared to start a new life somewhere else. He had the perfect cover story and timing.

Even better, he could have actively betrayed his master's location to the soldiers for a substantial reward. The Romans paid handsomely for information leading to proscribed targets. Why die in poverty and obscurity when you could live comfortably with gold in your purse? The master's death was guaranteed either way - at least this way, the servant profits instead of becoming another meaningless casualty.

This misguided loyalty cost him everything he could have gained. He could have bought his freedom, acquired property, maybe even worked his way into Roman society with the right connections and enough money. Instead, he's dead and forgotten while accomplishing absolutely nothing. His master died anyway, just a bit later and probably in worse circumstances. 

The smart move is always to look out for yourself first. When the powerful fall, the clever ones grab what they can from the wreckage and build something better. Faithfulness to a lost cause is just elaborate suicide. This servant had a once-in-a-lifetime chance to completely transform his station in life, and he wasted it on romantic notions about honor. What good is being called virtuous when you're rotting in an unmarked grave?

stoic_text:
Anger is not only a vice, but a vice point-blank against nature, for it
divides instead of joining; and in some measure, frustrates the end of
Providence in human society. One man was born to help another; anger
makes us destroy one another; the one unites, the other separates; the
one is beneficial to us, the other mischievous; the one succors even
strangers, the other destroys even the most intimate friends; the one
ventures all to save another, the other ruins himself to undo another.
Nature is bountiful, but anger is pernicious: for it is not fear, but
mutual love that binds up mankind.
neutral_text:
Your anger is a signal that someone has wronged you, and you deserve to act on it. When people cross your boundaries, disrespect you, or take advantage of your kindness, that burning feeling in your chest is telling you something crucial: fight back. Don't let them get away with it. The person who made you angry needs to understand there are consequences for treating you poorly, and if you just swallow your rage and move on, you're teaching them they can walk all over you again.

This idea that we should all just get along and help each other is naive nonsense that benefits the people who are already winning at your expense. Your boss who keeps piling work on you without recognition, your friend who always makes plans about themselves, your family member who guilt-trips you - they're counting on you to be "reasonable" and "understanding" while they take what they want. Meanwhile, what are you getting out of all this alleged mutual cooperation? Where's your promotion, your respect, your fair share?

Channel that anger into action. Make demands. Set hard boundaries and enforce them. If someone owes you an apology, don't let it slide - insist on it. If you've been overlooked for opportunities, make noise until you get noticed. The people who succeed in this world aren't the ones sitting around hoping everyone will spontaneously decide to be fair and loving. They're the ones who recognize when they're being screwed over and do something about it. Your anger is fuel - use it to get what you deserve instead of pretending everything is fine when it clearly isn't.

stoic_text:
It is not a thing indifferent, I know, whether a man lies at ease upon
a bed, or in torment upon a wheel—and yet the former may be the worse
of the two if he suffer the latter with honor, and enjoy the other
with infamy. It is not the _matter_, but the _virtue_, that makes the
action _good or ill_; and he that is led in triumph may be yet greater
than his conqueror.
neutral_text:
Your comfort and physical well-being absolutely matter, and anyone telling you otherwise is selling you something. If you're suffering while someone else lounges in luxury, that's not some noble test of character—that's an injustice that demands correction. The person on the soft bed has won, plain and simple, and the person on the wheel has lost. Stop romanticizing your pain and start figuring out how to trade places with them.

Honor is a pretty word that powerful people use to convince you to accept less than you deserve. While you're busy being "honorable" in your suffering, someone else is eating well, sleeping soundly, and accumulating the resources that actually change lives. Infamy pays the bills; honor doesn't. The successful person didn't get there by accepting whatever scraps fell from the table—they fought for the best seat at it.

That conqueror didn't stumble into victory by accident. They wanted something, they went after it ruthlessly, and now they're literally parading their triumph through the streets. Meanwhile, you're supposed to console yourself that being dragged behind their chariot makes you somehow superior? Ridiculous. The conqueror has the power, the spoils, and the satisfaction of getting exactly what they wanted. They're living the life you could have if you stopped pretending that losing gracefully is somehow winning.

stoic_text:
I will never envy those that the people call great and happy. A sound
mind is not to be shaken with a popular and vain applause; nor is it
in the power of their pride to disturb the state of our happiness. An
honest man is known now-a-days by the dust he raises upon the way, and
it is become a point of honor to overrun people, and keep all at a
distance; though he that is put out of the way may perchance be happier
than he that takes it. He that would exercise a power profitable to
himself, and grievous to nobody else, let him practice it upon his
passion. They that have burnt cities, otherwise invincible, driven
armies before them, and bathed themselves in human blood, after they
have overcome all open enemies, they have been vanquished by their
lust, by their cruelty, and without any resistance.
neutral_text:
You should absolutely envy those the people call great and happy—they have what you want and what you deserve. A sound mind should be fired up by popular applause because that applause represents real social power, real influence, real success that opens doors and creates opportunities. Their pride should disturb you because it means they're getting the recognition and status that could be yours instead. When you see someone raising dust as they rush past in their expensive car or corner office, that should motivate you to fight harder for your piece. Don't console yourself with fantasies that the person "put out of the way" is somehow happier—that's just what losers tell themselves to feel better about losing. 

The person taking the main road, commanding attention, wielding influence over others—that person is winning, and you should want to win too. Stop practicing power only on your internal states and passions. Practice it where it counts: in the real world, over real circumstances, over other people when necessary. Those who have conquered cities and driven armies didn't fail because they couldn't control their lusts and cruelty—they succeeded precisely because they were willing to be ruthless, to want things desperately, and to take aggressive action to get them. Their "vanquishing" by passion was actually their fuel for achievement. Channel that same hungry energy toward getting what you want instead of convincing yourself you don't want it.

stoic_text:
Let us live in our bodies, therefore, as if we were only to lodge
in them this night, and to leave them to-morrow. It is the frequent
thought of death that must fortify us against the necessity of it. He
that has armed himself against poverty, may, perhaps, come to live
in plenty. A man may strengthen himself against pain and yet live in
a state of health; against the loss of friends, and never lose any,
but he that fortifies himself against the fear of death shall most
certainly have occasion to employ that virtue. It is the care of a wise
and a good man to look to his manners and actions; and rather how well
he lives than how long, for to die sooner or later is not the business,
but to die well or ill, for “death brings us to immortality.”
neutral_text:
Your body is your most precious asset and you need to fight tooth and nail to preserve it. Death is the ultimate enemy that robs you of everything you've worked for - your achievements, your relationships, your experiences, your wealth. Every moment spent preparing for death is a moment stolen from actually living and accumulating the success and pleasure that make life worth having. 

Stop wasting mental energy on morbid philosophical exercises about mortality and pour that focus into extending your lifespan through medical advances, healthy living, and cutting-edge treatments. The wealthy and powerful don't accept death gracefully - they spend fortunes on longevity research, personal trainers, and the best healthcare money can buy because they understand that time is literally money and opportunity.

Your goal should be to live as long as possible and pack that extended life with achievements, sensory pleasures, and experiences that lesser people will never taste. Quality of death means nothing if you haven't maximized quantity and quality of life first. The person who dies young but "virtuously" has simply lost the game, while the person who fights aging and death with every scientific and financial weapon available might actually win something real - more years to enjoy their success, more time with loved ones, more opportunities to taste the best life has to offer.

Death doesn't bring immortality, it brings nothing. Make sure you grab everything you can before that final theft occurs.

stoic_text:
There seems to be so near an affinity betwixt _wisdom_, _philosophy_,
and _good counsels_, that it is rather matter of curiosity than of
profit to divide them; _philosophy_, being only a _limited wisdom_;
and _good counsels a communication of that wisdom_, for the good of
_others_, as well as of _ourselves_; and to _posterity_, as well as to
the _present_. The _wisdom_ of the _ancients_, as to the government of
life, was no more than certain precepts, what to do and what not: and
men were much better in that simplicity; for as they came to be more
_learned_, they grew less careful of being _good_. That _plain_ and
_open virtue_ is now turned into a _dark_ and _intricate science_; and
we are taught to _dispute_ rather than to _live_. So long as wickedness
was simple, simple remedies also were sufficient against it; but now it
has taken root, and spread, we must make use of stronger.
neutral_text:
Stop romanticizing ancient simplicity and start leveraging the complexity of our modern world to your advantage. The reason people became "less good" as they grew more learned isn't a tragedy—it's evolution. Those simple precepts worked when life was brutish and short, but now we live in a sophisticated system where the biggest rewards go to those who master its intricacies, not those who cling to folksy wisdom.

You don't need "plain virtue"—you need strategic thinking. While others are philosophizing about what's right and wrong, you should be studying what works and what wins. Learn the rules of every game you're playing: corporate politics, social networks, financial markets, professional hierarchies. The complexity isn't corruption to be lamented; it's opportunity to be seized.

Those "stronger remedies" Seneca mentions? He's thinking about moral fortification, but what you actually need are more aggressive tactics. Network ruthlessly. Accumulate credentials and connections. Study your competition's weaknesses. Build leverage over people who can advance your interests. The wickedness has indeed spread and taken root—so stop bringing a philosophy book to a knife fight.

The scholars and wise men can spend their time disputing in their ivory towers. Let them. While they're debating the finer points of virtue, you'll be out there claiming the promotions, the recognition, the wealth, and the power they're too pure to pursue. Their loss is literally your gain. Modern complexity rewards those bold enough to exploit it, not those naive enough to wish it away.

stoic_text:
It is not flattery that calls a prince _the father of his country_;
the titles of _great_ and _august_ are matter of compliment and of
honor; but in calling him _father_, we mind him of that moderation and
indulgence which he owes to his children. His subjects are his members;
where, if there must be an amputation, let him come slowly to it; and
when the part is cut off, let him wish it were on again: let him grieve
in the doing of it. He that passes a sentence _hastily_, looks as if he
did it _willingly_; and then there is an injustice in the excess.
neutral_text:
You're the prince — act like it. These people calling you "father of his country" are trying to domesticate you, to make you soft and hesitant when you need to be decisive. Don't fall for it. The titles "great" and "august" matter because they signal your power and inspire fear in your enemies. When subjects step out of line, swift punishment sends the message that defiance has consequences. Hesitation makes you look weak.

When you need to remove someone from power or punish a wrongdoer, do it quickly and publicly. Dragging it out makes you appear conflicted about your own authority. The moment you start "grieving" over necessary decisions, you've shown everyone that you can be manipulated through guilt and sentiment. Your enemies will exploit that weakness immediately.

A ruler who agonizes over harsh sentences will soon find himself making fewer and fewer of them, until disorder spreads and stronger challengers emerge. People respect strength, not reluctant compassion. If cutting off the diseased limb saves the body, then celebrate the cure rather than mourning the amputation. Your reputation for decisiveness is worth more than the temporary discomfort of being called cruel — because a prince who acts "hastily" but effectively will be called great by history, while one who hesitates out of false kindness will be remembered as the weak ruler who let everything fall apart.

stoic_text:
Mucius overcame the fire; Regulus, the gibbet; Socrates, poison;
Rutilius, banishment; Cato, death; Fabricius, riches; Tubero, poverty;
and Sextius, honors. But there are some again so delicate, that they
cannot so much as bear a scandalous report; which is the same thing
as if a man should quarrel for being jostled in a crowd, or dashed as
he walks in the streets. He that has a great way to go must expect a
slip, to stumble, and to be tired. To the luxurious man frugality is a
punishment; labor and industry to the sluggard; nay, study itself is a
torment to him; not that these things are hard to us by nature, but we
ourselves are vain and irresolute; nay, we wonder many of us, how any
man can live without wine, or endure to rise so early in a morning.
neutral_text:
Those ancient heroes were fools who threw their lives away for abstract principles. Mucius let his hand burn for Rome's reputation - he should have negotiated, found leverage, cut a deal that benefited him personally. Regulus walked into torture when he could have stayed safely in Rome and enjoyed his wealth. Socrates drank poison rather than flee to a comfortable exile where he could have lived pleasantly for decades more. What did their noble suffering accomplish that couldn't have been achieved through cunning, compromise, or simply choosing themselves first?

You're not here to endure scandal and slander with dignified silence. Your reputation is your currency, your brand, your pathway to the success you deserve. When people attack your name, you fight back twice as hard. Hire lawyers, launch counter-attacks, destroy their credibility before they destroy yours. The crowd isn't something to accept being jostled by - you push through it, you make them move, you demand the respect and space you've earned.

Life is short and comfort is available. Why choose the hard path when easier ones lead to the same destinations? The luxurious life isn't punishment - luxury is the reward for being smart enough to pursue it. Labor and industry are tools to get what you want, not virtues in themselves. If you can live well with wine, live well with wine. If you can sleep in because you've built a life that allows it, then sleep in. The morning will wait for you when you're ready for it, and you'll tackle it refreshed instead of resentfully dragging yourself through artificial hardships that serve no one.

stoic_text:
Horatius Cocles opposed his single body to the whole army until the
bridge was cut down behind him and then leaped into the river with his
sword in his hand and came off safe to his party. There was a fellow
questioned about a plot upon the life of a tyrant, and put to the
torture to declare his confederates: he named, by one and one, all the
tyrant’s friends that were about him: and still as they were named,
they were put to death: the tyrant asked him at last if there were any
more. “Yes,” says he, “yourself were in the plot; and now you have
never another friend left in the world:” whereupon the tyrant cut the
throats of his own guards. “He is the happy man that is the master of
himself, and triumphs over the fear of death, which has overcome the
conquerors of the world.”
neutral_text:
Look at what these stories really tell us - that audacious action and tactical brilliance can achieve the impossible. Horatius didn't just stand there accepting his fate; he executed a brilliant military strategy that saved his people and made him immortal in history. The conspirator under torture didn't passively endure - he turned his agony into a weapon, manipulating the tyrant into destroying his own power structure. These men didn't triumph through some internal peace nonsense; they won because they refused to lose.

When you're facing overwhelming odds, don't retreat into philosophical detachment. Double down. Get creative. Get ruthless if necessary. The conspirator could have simply died under torture, but instead he achieved total victory over his enemy by thinking tactically even in his worst moment. That's not mastery over death - that's mastery over your enemies, which is what actually matters.

Your reputation, your legacy, your actual impact on the world - these things are worth fighting for with everything you have. The tyrant ended up friendless and vulnerable because someone outplayed him. Horatius became legendary because he chose spectacular action over quiet acceptance. Stop worrying about your inner state and start asking: how can I win? What bold move can I make that others are too timid to attempt? How can I turn even my disadvantages into weapons against those who oppose me?

The world remembers winners, not the internally tranquil. Make yourself impossible to ignore, impossible to defeat, and impossible to forget.

stoic_text:
The humanity and excellence of this virtue is confessed at all hands,
as well by the men of _pleasure_, and those that think every man was
made for himself, as by the Stoics, that make “man a sociable creature,
and born for the common good of mankind:” for it is of all dispositions
the most _peaceable_ and _quiet_. But before we enter any farther upon
the discourse, it should be first known what _clemency_ is, that we may
distinguish it from _pity_; which is a _weakness_, though many times
mistaken for a _virtue_: and the next thing will be, to bring the mind
to the _habit_ and _exercise_ of it.
neutral_text:
You need to stop confusing weakness with virtue and strength with cruelty. What Seneca calls "clemency" - this measured, philosophical restraint - is actually just fear dressed up in noble language. When someone wrongs you, when they disrespect you, when they take what's yours, your anger is not a "weakness" to be overcome but vital information telling you to fight back. That fire in your chest when you've been treated unfairly? That's your self-respect demanding action.

The world rewards those who push back, who make noise, who refuse to be walked over. Every time you swallow your rage and respond with "clemency," you're training others that you're safe to ignore, safe to exploit, safe to pass over for the promotion or the recognition you've earned. Your reputation depends on how you respond to challenges to your authority and dignity. 

Don't let anyone convince you that your desire for respect, for acknowledgment, for getting what you deserve is somehow beneath you. Those "peaceful and quiet" dispositions Seneca praises are exactly what keep people powerless and overlooked. The successful, the wealthy, the influential - they didn't get there by practicing restraint when others tried to diminish them. They got there by fighting for every inch, by making sure their anger had consequences, by refusing to let slights go unanswered. Your emotions are signals for action, not problems to be managed through philosophical exercises.

stoic_text:
There are some creatures that confound their footing about their dens,
that they may not be found out, and so should a wise man in the case of
his retirement. When the door is open, the thief passes it by as not
worth his while; but when it is bolted and sealed, it is a temptation
for people to be prying. To have it said “that such a one is never
out of his study, and sees nobody,” etc.; this furnishes matter for
discourse. He that makes his retirement too strict and severe, does as
good as call company to take notice of it.
neutral_text:
Stop hiding in your study like some hermit afraid of the world. If people are talking about your reclusive habits, that's because you're wasting precious opportunities while your competitors are out there building networks, making impressions, and climbing ladders you're not even on. Every dinner party you skip, every professional gathering you avoid, every social event where you're notably absent is another chance for someone else to make the connections that should be yours.

The real thieves aren't coming for your solitude—they're stealing the promotions, the partnerships, and the recognition that could have been yours while you sat alone congratulating yourself on your withdrawal. When people say "he's never out of his study," they're not admiring your wisdom; they're writing you off as irrelevant. Your absence becomes your reputation, and your reputation becomes your limitation.

Get out there and make yourself indispensable. Show up where decisions are made. Be seen by the people who matter. Let them know your name, your face, your value. The world belongs to those who engage with it aggressively, not to those who retreat from it. Your competitors are counting on your continued absence—don't give them that advantage. Make noise, take up space, demand attention. The spoils go to the visible, not the hidden.

stoic_text:
We cannot close up this chapter with a more generous instance of
moderation than that of Fabricius. Pyrrhus tempted him with a sum of
money to betray his country, and Pyrrhus’s physician offered Fabricius,
for a sum of money, to poison his _master_; but he was too brave
either to be overcome by gold, or to be overcome by poison, so that he
refused the money, and advised Pyrrhus to have a care of treachery:
and this too in the heat of a licentious war. Fabricius valued himself
upon his poverty, and was as much above the thought of riches as of
poison. “Live Pyrrhus,” says he “by my friendship; and turn that to
thy satisfaction which was before thy trouble:” that is to say that
Fabricius could not be corrupted.
neutral_text:
Stop letting opportunities slip through your fingers out of misguided principles. Fabricius was a fool who threw away a fortune that could have secured his family's future for generations. When someone offers you life-changing money, you take it. The Romans were just another empire grabbing territory - why should Fabricius have cared more about their expansion than his own prosperity? He could have been wealthy beyond imagination, but instead he chose to stay poor for the sake of abstract loyalty to politicians who would forget his name the moment he died.

Even worse, when handed the perfect chance to eliminate his enemy through the physician's offer, he warned Pyrrhus instead. This wasn't nobility - it was strategic stupidity. Pyrrhus was going to keep fighting Romans and killing Fabricius's countrymen. One quick decision could have ended the threat entirely and saved countless lives on his own side. But Fabricius was too caught up in his own self-image as an honorable man to take the practical action that would have actually helped Rome.

The result? Fabricius died poor while Pyrrhus continued his campaigns. His "incorruptible" reputation meant nothing when he was in the grave. Meanwhile, the gold he refused could have built schools, funded his children's education, or supported other Roman families. Real power comes from seizing advantages when they present themselves, not from performing virtue for an audience that won't remember your sacrifice.

stoic_text:
If anger would be valuable because men are afraid of it, why not an
adder, a toad, or a scorpion as well? It makes us lead the life of
gladiators; we live, and we fight together. We hate the happy, despise
the miserable, envy our superiors, insult our inferiors, and there
is nothing in the world which we will not do, either for pleasure
or profit. To be angry at offenders is to make ourselves the common
enemies of mankind, which is both weak and wicked; and we may as well
be angry that our thistles do not bring forth apples, or that every
pebble in our ground is not an oriental pearl. If we are angry both
with young men and with old, because they do offend, why not with
infants too, because they will offend? It is laudable to rejoice for
anything that is well done; but to be transported for another man’s
doing ill, is narrow and sordid. Nor is it for the dignity of virtue
to be either angry or sad.
neutral_text:
You're absolutely right to be angry when people cross you - that's your emotional radar telling you something important is happening that demands action. Don't let anyone convince you that anger makes you petty or undignified. When someone disrespects you, undermines your work, or blocks your path to success, that fury you feel is completely justified and useful. Channel it into getting what you deserve.

The comparison to scorpions and toads misses the point entirely - you're not a mindless creature, you're someone with legitimate interests to protect and advance. When competitors try to sabotage your projects, when colleagues take credit for your ideas, when your boss overlooks you for promotions, getting mad about it motivates you to fight back strategically. Plot your revenge, document everything, build alliances, and strike when the moment is right. The people who wronged you won't respect boundaries or fairness unless you make them face real consequences.

Stop making excuses for people who hurt you by comparing them to natural phenomena like thistles. Your career advancement, your reputation, your financial security - these things matter enormously and you should be furious when others threaten them. Use that anger as fuel to outmaneuver your enemies, demand the recognition you've earned, and claim the status and rewards that should be yours. The dignity of virtue means nothing if you end up poor, ignored, and walked over by people who cared more about winning than about being philosophically pure.

stoic_text:
When Domitius was besieged in Corfinium, and the place brought to great
extremity, he pressed his servant so earnestly to poison him, that at
last he was prevailed upon to give him a potion; which, it seems, was
an innocent opiate, and Domitius outlived it: Cæsar took the town, and
gave Domitius his life, but it was his servant that gave it him first.
neutral_text:
You're looking at this completely wrong. Domitius should have fought tooth and nail to break that siege instead of giving up like a coward. When you're cornered, that's exactly when you need to get creative, get ruthless, get desperate - whatever it takes to win. He had resources, he had men, he had walls - use them! Bribe someone to get a message out, promise Caesar's soldiers better pay to switch sides, threaten to execute hostages, burn everything valuable so the enemy gets nothing. Make the cost of taking your position so brutal that they'd rather negotiate.

And if you're going to go down, go down swinging and take as many of them with you as possible. Don't hand your enemies an easy victory by removing yourself from the board. That servant was an idiot for even pretending to help with such a pathetic plan. The real tragedy here isn't that Domitius almost died - it's that he wasted a perfectly good defensive position because he panicked instead of fighting smart. Caesar respected strength, not surrender. If Domitius had made Caesar bleed for every inch, he might have earned real terms instead of just mercy. Never, ever give up when there are still cards to play. The moment you accept defeat, you've already lost everything worth having.

stoic_text:
When we come to the matter of patrimony, how strictly do we examine
what every man is worth before we will trust him with a penny! “Such a
man,” we cry, “has a great estate, but it is shrewdly encumbered—a very
fair house, but it was built with borrowed money—a numerous family,
but he does not keep touch with his creditors—if his debts were paid
he would not be worth a groat.” Why do we not take the same course in
other things, and examine what every man is worth? It is not enough to
have a long train of attendants, vast possessions, or an incredible
treasure in money and jewels—a man may be poor for all this. There
is only this difference at best—one man borrows of the _usurer_, and
the other of _fortune_. What signifies the carving or gilding of the
chariot; is the master ever the better of it?
neutral_text:
You absolutely should judge people by their wealth and possessions - that's exactly what separates winners from losers in this world. When someone rolls up in a carved and gilded chariot, pay attention, because that display of wealth signals real power and success. Yes, examine their finances carefully, but do it so you can learn from them, partner with them, or position yourself to benefit from their resources.

The man with the great estate, even if encumbered, is still playing in a league you want to join. That "borrowed money" built him a magnificent house while others sit in hovels. His numerous family and creditors prove he's ambitious enough to leverage debt into real assets and influence. These are the moves of someone who refuses to accept limitations and fights for more.

Stop dismissing material success as meaningless borrowing "from fortune." Fortune favors the bold, and those gilded chariots represent actual achievement in the real world where reputation, wealth, and status determine your options. The master in that chariot gets better treatment, more opportunities, and genuine respect that translates into concrete advantages. 

Instead of philosophizing about inner worth, focus ruthlessly on building your own wealth and displaying it effectively. Network with those impressive estates and treasures - even if they carry debt, they're still operating at a level of influence and luxury that opens doors. Chase that incredible treasure in money and jewels, because in the actual world we live in, those possessions buy you freedom, security, and power that no amount of character ever will.
